planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to dry, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; H. villosa is the go-to heuchera for the humid Southeast because it tolerates heat, summer humidity, and even short dry spells far better than Pacific coast and garden hybrids.

directions: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to dry, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; H. villosa is the go-to heuchera for the humid Southeast because it tolerates heat, summer humidity, and even short dry spells far better than the Pacific coast and garden hybrids. The large, softly hairy, maple-shaped leaves in shades of green to bronze form bold mounds in shaded borders, and airy wands of tiny white flowers rise 18–24 inches in late summer, providing nectar for small native bees. It makes an outstanding evergreen groundcover under deciduous trees and is deer-resistant; divide clumps every 3–4 years in spring to renew vigor.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; seeds cold-stratify naturally over winter and germinate in spring.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; seeds cold-stratify naturally over winter and germinate in spring. Fringe cups is a Pacific Northwest native perennial in the saxifrage family that forms semi-evergreen rosettes of rounded, softly hairy, heart-shaped leaves and sends up elegant, 18–30 inch racemes of small, urn-shaped flowers whose petals are deeply fringed, opening white and aging to pink and red, in spring and early summer. It spreads modestly by seed and rhizomes to naturalize into a graceful woodland groundcover that pairs beautifully with ferns, bleeding heart, and tiarella. Tolerates deep shade under conifers.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in poor to average, well-drained, dry to moderately moist soil in partial to full sun; lamb's ears struggles in wet, humid conditions — ensure excellent drainage, especially in the humid South, and do not mulch around the crowns.

directions: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in poor to average, well-drained, dry to moderately moist soil in partial to full sun; lamb's ears struggles in wet, humid conditions — ensure excellent drainage, especially in the humid South, and do not mulch around the crowns. Stachys byzantina is grown almost entirely for its spectacular dense-woolly, silver-white, softly tactile leaves that form a spreading, mat-forming groundcover and make an elegant foil for colorful perennials; in late spring to summer it sends up fuzzy, silver-lavender flower spikes that some gardeners remove to keep the foliage display clean. Divide every 2–3 years in early spring to remove dead centers and maintain a tidy mat. Excellent at the front of borders, edging paths, and as dry-garden groundcover.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, average to sandy, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; established plants are drought-tolerant and require little supplemental care in zones 7–10.

directions: Direct sow in spring in well-drained, average to sandy, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; established plants are drought-tolerant and require little supplemental care in zones 7–10. Carolina wild petunia is a slender-stemmed native perennial with opposite, oblong leaves and a succession of lavender-blue, funnel-shaped flowers 1.5 inches across from spring through fall; individual flowers last only one day but are produced continuously over a long season. It is a specialist host and nectar plant for the common buckeye butterfly and the Malachite butterfly in southern Texas and Florida. The plant self-seeds moderately and gradually naturalizes into open, sandy, or rocky woodland edges and roadsides; in zone 9–10 it persists as a semi-woody subshrub.

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown transplants or rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; white baneberry is very slow from seed (requiring two winters to break double dormancy) — purchase nursery stock only.

directions: Plant nursery-grown transplants or rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; white baneberry is very slow from seed (requiring two winters to break double dormancy) — purchase nursery stock only. Actaea pachypoda is a bold native perennial of eastern North American deciduous forests, producing large, compound, deeply toothed leaves and cylindrical clusters of white flowers with distinctive stamens in spring, followed by the plant's most dramatic feature: clusters of pure white berries, each with a prominent black dot, on thick red pedicels that remain on the plant into October, creating an eerie, memorable display. All parts of the plant — especially the berries — are highly toxic; the berries can cause cardiac arrest if eaten and must be kept away from children. An outstanding specimen for shaded native plant gardens, it is one of the most distinctive-looking native perennials in late summer.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; seeds cold-stratify over winter and germinate in early spring.

directions: Direct sow fresh seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; seeds cold-stratify over winter and germinate in early spring. Canada sanicle is a native biennial to short-lived perennial of eastern North American forests and woodland edges, producing deeply palmate, shiny dark-green leaves and small clusters of tiny white flowers in early summer that are excellent bee forage; it is a specialist forage plant for native mining bees and sweat bees. The plant typically self-seeds in suitable conditions and gradually naturalizes under deciduous trees, particularly in rich mesic forest soils alongside native ferns and woodland wildflowers. Use it as an unobtrusive but ecologically valuable filler in a native shade garden.
